speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing survivor Dr. Esther Fox, interviewer, Miryam Rabner. We're in New York City, United States of America. The interview is being conducted in English. Tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe you want a cup of coffee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, no. OK, so the truck stopped. And you came to a new place.

speaker: interviewee
text: To a new place. I don't recall now at all, somehow, where we came, how it-- I don't remember. And we-- here, we were divided, this, our transport, I think so, in one barrack. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember. No, I'm sorry, I made a mistake. I told somebody that I am a doctor. And the other two, from Yugoslavia. And they told us that there is a place where-- oh no, there, now I know. The barracks, but each barrack has a, a supervisor, the eldest, a Jewish, and had helper who provide the food and everything. And over there, I will be asleep, the doctor. Because some doctor, this Hungarian, went to the chief, to [? Bimko, ?] Hadassah [? Bimko. ?] She's [NON-ENGLISH]. You heard already, the one I mentioned. She lived there. But I didn't have this luck. And I was assigned to a certain barrack. I don't remember the name, but-- and I lived with the personnel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me, to this barrack. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: With prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are not [INAUDIBLE]. We are not prisoners, yes. But with a special room. But they were on the [? perches. ?] And here were also [? perch. ?] But--

speaker: interviewee
text: So right away, I didn't have where to sleep. Because they didn't accommodate me too much, too better. And all right, wherever, on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember. After a few days, shortly, I became right everybody. Not I, the whole thing with louse, from the top to the belly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came one day in the beginning, I said I found a louse. So they already-- I was a leper. A leper, they-- I said, but what, could there be everybody sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: But one, from Poland, she's here with us. She said, sleep next to me. She had the place. And later, I went-- I wanted to see my transport, what happened to them, my Hungarian--

speaker: interviewer
text: To your girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- no, all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, to your girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I looked. I didn't know where they are. But I found out. They told me, I came. I didn't recognize none of them. They changed so much because they had it not so bad we had. And especially these who worked in the kitchen, they ate good. And I met these, not recognized them at all. They always sick. Yet my adopted [? thing, ?] I found her. So I got her to my thing. And she had frostbites on the toes. So first of all, it was gangrene, completely black. And there's no anesthesia, no nothing. I told here, a dish from the soup. I took, as my surgical instrument-- and I cut her off the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Toes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The toes. It doesn't hurt her even. And this was my-- the dish, the tragedy of everything. And she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And later, after the war finished, from Poland, from Lodz, a doctor wrote to me. I wrote to them that somehow, that her mother wants to go to Germany to look for her. And I didn't dare to write to her. I told to this doctor-- he was the, the president of the Jewish physician-- to tell the mother that I was with her, and I know she is dead, and let not her go to look for her. So this is my-- the tragedy, I mix everything what come later and now. So now--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do in Bergen-Belsen every single day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was like a-- first of all, I got sick. After two weeks-- incubation period of typhus is two weeks. So I got sick myself. And to mention it is Jewish, nobody-- there was a nurse who she had typhus. She's recovered from typhus. She never gave me a sip of water. And sure, my portion of bread, I couldn't eat. So I told when I recover, she will keep it. Not keep my bread, will be old, but she will shift it. And I have my, my, my account over there. No, never. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- I-- the 15th of February, '44, I took sick. '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. Or we came to Bergen-Belsen the 15th of February, '45. We arrived. And right after I got two weeks, I got sick, or something, or got sick the 15th, because it's two weeks. Whatever. And I was very weak. When I, like to say, recovered, I couldn't walk. So I was moving on my buttocks. I couldn't walk. But gradually-- but this was later. But being so weak that I couldn't walk of-- at all, came news. Somebody ran in. We are liberated. The British are here, the army. It was true. But I was so, such a pessimist, I said, listen, don't you believe. These are German who put on other colored uniform. We should jubiliate. They should have a reason to kill us. But these were the British. And we were liberated the 15th of April. Now I have it. The 15th of February I arrived there. And the 15th of April was the day when we were liberated by the British army. And that time, I still couldn't walk, but a little better. And this doctor from Yugoslavia, Ruth, she knew English. So she was going with them. And she told me-- I already later could join them. I was a little stronger-- that they had big supplies of DDT to delouse us. But they didn't use it on us. They had it, but they let everybody-- and the supervisor, the German, who was the supervisor in Guben Guben and went with us to, to, to Bergen-Belsen, she died. She got Guben too. The louse didn't select her for naught. So I just say, instead to use the DDT, they didn't. This what they wanted. And they came out that they intended to dynamite the whole camp-- but they didn't make it, the British came a day earlier-- because they didn't want to leave any trace. And so this we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I will tell you a little bit about-- the British army wanted very much to help, to do. And they were in a shock. It's another story, I'm not talking about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we told them not to give so much food and not this kind of food. We have conferences like with them. We said the stomach cannot take anything. And they gave all the cans with beans, with [INAUDIBLE]. So thousands died from the overfeeding, because the stomach couldn't take it. And the end of everything was not the end. The going on, piles, piles, mountains of, of, of cadavers-- mountains, mountains. And this was shifted up, down, legs, hands, the foot. Nobody cared. This is one more body, but thousands of bodies. And the few doctors which were in Bergen-Belsen, the British took us to places where before was a German hospital. So first they took the couple doctors to-- we should settle, before they brought patients.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember, they came. He was a French parachuter, it's because I recognized in those days such a red beret. And the other doctor who was capable of moving-- because of my close friends, she couldn't, she stayed still longer.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had the little bundle. I don't know what. He took it from here. And she said to me in French, I feel a lady. He holds my bundle. And he happened to be French, or understand French. So he had tears in his eyes. Here she was-- she's in Nice. I visit her later in France, in Nice-- that he felt a woman, a doctor, then he said, she had the bundle, probably a piece of her rug, nothing. And he kept it. So he had tears in his eyes. How our dignity of a person was so lowered that she says I feel a woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took me to my room. And she had another building, where she was supposed to be the doctor. And on the table were some crackers and chocolate. I wouldn't know, what's-- I thought I'm crazy. I don't know what's going on, to see such a thing, such a-- So and they were very nice. And we had conferences. And they started bringing patients. So there was one patient of mine, she from my hometown. She looked like a 60-years-old woman, with such a big belly and the umbilicus sticking out like that, because she had [INAUDIBLE]. This is called hunger edema.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said that she is 22 years old. And she is from the Lodz ghetto. And she knows me. And she says she knows my brother. He was sick once in the ghetto and was in the hospital. I said this cannot be. She looks like a 60-year-old woman. But she worked in the hospital in a different than I, in another speciality. So I called a friend, a doctor. And I said, she talks the name. I want to know whether she knows what she's talking or not. And she talked, she says yes, I know her. She is. And she recovered. And she a beauty. And she was 20 years old. She lives in Israel, a beauty. But I didn't believe her. I thought she doesn't know what she's talking, the other, to tell me whether I can trust here. In the treatment doesn't matter, but to trust her. Later she became my nurse in Sweden. So this is by the way the good news.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Bergen-Belsen, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was one, in the army, from the Israel [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] brigade, Israel brigade, and is a Jew. And his name was Deutsch. And we spoke German all the time. I didn't know English. We even with the English people. So if was mix up Deutsch, [INAUDIBLE] Deutsch. The others didn't know that he's a Jew. But this is nothing. So later, we tried to what we could, to do for the patient. But many died because of the food. Because these who had to die from the typhus died anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for but-- but the food, they didn't listen to us. They didn't have the facilities either, to make diet cereal or whatever, tapioca. So it was bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, about in June, maybe, they came and they told us-- no. Once came some military men. They didn't look like British. They wore a uniform, a little different color. They were tall, there were blond. So I came to my people. I-- and it was just where I was. I said, I don't know who they are. They are not British, they are not German. But who are they? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were Swedish. And I never saw a Swede in my eye, in my eyes. So I didn't know, tall, blond. Now I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So later, we came out that the Swedish government offered to take 10,000 such refugees for rehabilitation, to, to take to Sweden. And they came to us with this proposition that we can choose. So there were a couple doctors, Polish, and a dentist with her daughter, and this and that. So we decided what to do. To Poland, out of question. I wouldn't go there. I wasn't connected with Israel. I didn't have no, no anything, and you couldn't go. So we said, let's four go to Sweden. Because it is a-- this we knew, we were educated, the older. It's a neutral country, it is a window open to the world. Let's try, we don't know. And I didn't have any relatives. Did you try to find your relatives after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't try. Whom? My mother, I knew later. When I was in Auschwitz and they told me that this is a crematorium and we felt the smell of hair, of bones, so I said no. This is impossible. They just burn. Because you couldn't believe these things. I was there. My mother was there. And now I find out later my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't think. And even to look, you also has, where should I look? From Sweden, it will be easier. If they want to rehabilitate, they will give us the help. So we decided we will go. So we went to Sweden. And I remember-- How did you go? In a--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a boat. And the boat in front of our boat was another boat, to be sure that it isn't mined with mines, to be sure, we came to Lubeck.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in your boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few. I don't remember exactly. But my friends were.

speaker: interviewer
text: A hundred people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I wouldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But my friends, we were about 10 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you clothes, decent clothing to change into?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know in Sweden, they, they unloused us, because they wouldn't let us in with the, with the lice. So there-- maybe twice, wait, I have to think of.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I want to say that it was right after the war. So it was a boat before ours that, to be sure that there isn't a mine, which will explode and will not kill us now by going. So we came to Sweden. And we came-- I don't remember exactly. But in Landskrona, we were on a quarantine. And over there, I remember, they unloused us what they could, to make sure we are clean from the louse.

speaker: interviewee
text: But how did they let us in with the louse, I don't remember. No, in Bergen-Belsen, you couldn't make it. So whatever, it was I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were three weeks on a quarantine. And later, they took us all up north, to Bargo. Because they couldn't take all of us to big cities. They couldn't accommodate, and the language and emaciated, and sick, and so and so. So we came to over there. And in meanwhile in Landskrona, they, they tried to be very kind to us, very much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Here, I remember to show the humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Landskrona, they gave us a, a little money, how do you say, pocket money, to everybody. So the younger, they bought candies, whatever. We kept the money. And after some-- before we were supposed to go north, we told the, the management that we want to go to Malmo. That we saved our-- the pocket money, and we will go for a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When did you get to Sweden? Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In July. The day, I don't know. But it was in July.

speaker: interviewee
text: So not only they allowed us to go-- we were two doctors, a dentist with her daughter, and another dentist, and a nurse-- so our group. So not only they allowed us to go-- we had our couple of whatever-- but they gave us a basket for food. And they gave us car fare to go forth and back. So we are rich. We have money, our own. So we walked around in Malmo, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Malmo, I got an attack of real hysteria. Because all of a sudden, I am free. But I don't have to look back, nobody is behind me to hit me. And I was afraid. So from this freedom, all of a sudden, I got an attack of hysteria. And I run away in a corner. And I said, let me cry it out. This I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we are in Malmo. And we wanted to go to a coffee shop, to be big shots. But we didn't know how much is a, a pastry. So we went to [INAUDIBLE] and an older friend, she was a dentist. And we will ask.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, we were finished products. I was a doctor before. She was a dentist. And here I--

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were people. It must have been a holiday or what, maybe not. So we let everybody go, but nobody was there. So I was very mean. When it came to ask how much is a [INAUDIBLE], I run out, left her alone. But she was older. And she was-- she asked, how much is a pastry and a coffee? In English?

speaker: interviewee
text: In English, yeah. They all knew English. Or in German, excuse me. We manipulated-- know English if you didn't know German. They all speak German-- spoke.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she came out. And we counted all the money we had. And we had enough to go in for a pastry and a coffee. And we sat down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to rehabilitation. And first she asked how much, because we were afraid we didn't have enough. So we went back. And in Sweden, everything was nice, yeah. So in-- later we went to Bargo, on north. So came social workers. And everybody recovered. They sent to the sanatorium for TB and near Stockholm were sanatorium, to say which is a beauty, I said. She also had TB. She was, and everybody else, they give the good, the best care.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they, later on, they sent up social workers. Where did you live in Bargo?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a couple of houses. They had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Private houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There was a camp, such a camp. And they started, what profession anybody. So with dressmakers, hat makers, all the thing was easy. They found them job all around Sweden. And they send them out, that everything taken care. Now remain we, the couple doctors. This is always trouble. We have no time to, to, to-- no right to practice. And we don't know to do anything. So we were the real trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: So were a conference in Stockholm, ladies physicians volunteered, invited all refugee doctors to Stockholm for a convention. And each lady doctor took to her house one doctor, and to take care. So we were three without anybody to take care. So in Stockholm was a professor Eric Wolf, a Jew. And they knew that he  is involved for Jewish causes. And he was a professor of forensic medicine. So they approached him, the committee, whether he could accommodate the three, our doctors. And he was rich, by the way, too. So she said-- they said yes. And we were the three.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she rented a room next to her house-- because she couldn't put up three people-- to a lady who, meanwhile, made a little money. And we slept over there. And she came each morning and took us to the conference and treated us with everything. The food, this was provided. And that's all. And meanwhile, they opened a little station for such who still were not completely well, or others, not from my group, an hour from Stockholm. So they put me in this station. And the two other doctors also got some place.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in charge of, of that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of one. But I mean, the two friends of mine-- because we were three-- they assigned for them too. But I was lucky to be, to go to this, one hour from Stockholm. And meanwhile, this Professor Wolf and Mrs. Wolf got-- took me as, to rehabilitate. And I was their adopted rehabilitation person. So it's an hour from Stockholm. So when there was a holiday like a seder-- they made a seder-- or anything else, they invited me. They came. And I worked in-- yeah. So after a year being there-- or not a year, I don't remember-- they all got well, it finished. Again, I am--

speaker: interviewee
text: Unemployed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unemployed. So what to do? So Professor Wolf, in Stockholm, they have the right to make our archival work, like in the museum, in the library, to do research. And the government pays for this. But I am not this kind. So he made for me, in his laboratory, a job. I have here from him. And this was paid by the government. Anyhow, I worked. They taught. They showed me what to do. And I worked-- was very-- and he watched me, everything what's going on with me. And I worked until I went to America. So now comes the chapter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you decide to go to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we'll come. But I'm thinking something more of Sweden. They were very nice all together, the Swedish people at that time. In the laboratory, and everything, they tried, in German, learned a little. The girls learned faster. They went out on dates and they learned-- I learned later Swedish too, but I forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, yeah. Now, yeah. Now comes the preface to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Sweden, everybody started getting letters. And people from America came, relatives to the-- that and we made up our group-- we were from our hometown, not all, but-- that if anybody gets a letter and doesn't know the information, don't throw it away. Show me, show me. I will show-- maybe the other knows. Not to throw away, try help.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my nurse, the one, the 60-year-old, had here an uncle who-- she found him, she maybe remembered him. And she got once a letter from Mr. Fox.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I forgot to tell you that before I married, before the war, and I dated my husband. One day before we married, he said tomorrow evening, we are not meeting because a colleague from us goes to America, and we give him a farewell party. No ladies, no wife, no nobody. And I was happy. I wasn't married. Why should they evaluate me, I'm good for him, not good for him. I was happy. But I remembered, he said Fox goes to America. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she's got a letter from a Mr. Fox to her. It happened to be my husband. Wait, but I never knew him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked her, maybe she knew his wife. And he had two children, two daughters. She was young, didn't know. And this came to me. And I read, and I say Fox. And I had these, his children were my patients, happened to be. So I said, give me this letter. And I read it, studied it, and I wrote to him. And I wrote that Lilka, is the name, got your card. But I got it through her. And I can give you some information that I knew your wife and the two children. I was-- happened to be their doctor. And if you've don't hear from them, don't lose hope. I didn't want to say that everybody's so sick with TB. But you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you see them, in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, still in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the ghetto. Yeah, I lost them. I didn't know them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, the people are sick. But you have to have patience. And they will-- in Sweden, they give them-- and all over they give them now good care. And maybe you will hear from them. And I gave him my address where I am writing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after this, I got a letter from him that I am happy to-- yeah, and I said postscriptum, I think that-- I will tell you who I am. I married-- I forgot to tell you. This, my first husband was the best friend of his. And he told me, this is my best friend, and he goes to America. I said postscriptum, by the way, I will tell you who I am. I think I married Majerkiewitz, which I think was your best friend. I married him two months-- he didn't, he wasn't in Poland at that time. And he went to the army and--

speaker: interviewee
text: So right after this, I got a letter from him, immediately. He said that friends of my friends are my friends. And so you don't hesitate to be in touch with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he started writing. He was a good-- he could write beautifully. Not just blah, blah, but he had a good style. At that time, a little affection, a little appeal. And he's not a stranger, or else I never saw them and I never knew him. But he had the best friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we corresponded. And once he said, if you would like to come to America, maybe I can help you. Because there are many doctors from Poland who came before the war or the beginning of the war, the beginning of the war. And they all make out some [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- he said, I'm not a rich man. But I will be able to give you everyday a lollipop. He wrote an ice cream, because he didn't know a lollipop. I didn't want to go to America. I had here an uncle. But I didn't know him, and he never really corresponded. So I said, what will I come to America, and I will be his [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't respond to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we corresponded. You understand, he write letter. And he wrote, I never hear from my family there. And in-- this was '45, and in '47, I get from him a letter. Now cut?
